title: Case on Competence Dispute Concerning Board of Audit and Inspection’s Inspection of Official Duties with respect to Election Commi
text:
1Case on Competence Dispute Concerning Board of Audit and Inspection’s Inspection of 
Official Duties with respect to Election Commissions
[2023Hun-Ra5, February 27, 2025]
* First Draft
In this case, the Court held that Respondent (Board of Audit and Inspection)’s inspection 
of Claimant (National Election Commission)’s personnel management status, including 
recruitment, conducted from June 1, 2023 to February 25, 2025 was carried out without 
constitutional or statutory authority and thereby infringed Claimant’s authority to 
independently perform its functions.
Background of the Case
Around May 10, 2023, news media reported allegations that the children of Claimant’s 
Secretary General and Deputy Secretary General had received preferential treatment in 
connection with experience-based competitive recruitment at election commissions. 
As suspicions regarding the preferential hiring of the children of Claimant’s employees 
became a matter of public concern, Respondent, at a meeting of its Committee on May 31, 
2023, resolved to add “Personnel Management Status, Including Hiring at Election 
Commissions” to the matters subject to audit and inspection, and on June 1, 2023, requested 
Claimant to submit materials pursuant to Article 27 and other relevant provisions of the 
Board of Audit and Inspection Act in connection with the above matter.
In response, Claimant asserted that, since it is not an administrative agency as prescribed in 
Article 97 of the Constitution, it is not subject to Respondent’s inspections of official duties 
and therefore could not readily accept such inspections. Respondent, however, maintained 
that, under Article 24 of the Board of Audit and Inspection Act, Claimant is subject to such 
inspections and warned that, if Claimant failed to comply with the request for materials 
without just cause, it would take stern measures pursuant to Article 51 of the same Act.
On June 9, 2023, Claimant, in order to promptly dispel the controversy surrounding the 
alleged preferential hiring of children and to focus on preparations for the upcoming general 
election, expressed its willingness to accept Respondent’s audit and inspection. However, 
Claimant also stated that it would file a competence dispute case to clarify the scope of 
2Respondent’s audit and inspection.
Thereafter, on July 28, 2023, Claimant filed the instant competence dispute case, claiming 
that the audit and inspection titled “Personnel Management Status, Including Hiring at 
Election Commissions,” which Respondent had been conducting with respect to Claimant 
since June 1, 2023, was conducted against Claimant ––an entity that cannot be subject to 
Respondent’s inspection of official duties ––and thereby infringed its authority.
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether the inspections of official duties 
concerning “Personnel Management Practices, Including Hiring at the National Election 
Commission” (hereinafter referred to as the “Inspections at Issue”), which Respondent 
conducted with respect to Claimant from June 1, 2023 to February 25, 2025 infringed 
Claimant’s authority granted under the Constitution or statutes.
Summary of the Decision
1. Claimant’s authority to perform its duties independently 
The functions of elections are possible only when their freedom and fairness are 
guaranteed. If elections and voting are not managed fairly, they cannot fulfill their inherent 
democratic political functions and will be reduced to a mere formality. To this end, the 
election commission must be able to perform independently not only the duties of election 
management themselves, but also various tasks relating to, inter alia, personnel, 
organizational operations, and internal discipline that are essential to carrying out those duties 
fairly. 
In this regard, the Constitution and the Election Commission Act establish Claimant as an 
independent constitutional organ, grant it authority over election affairs and the power to 
enact rules concerning internal discipline, and provide that Claimant may directly regulate 
matters relating to the various affairs incidental to the operation of the election management 
agency, such as personnel and organizational operations.
2. Claimant’s status as an independent constitutional organ 
The Constitution of the First Republic did not contain a provision expressly establishing an 
3election management body, and election management duties were performed by the election 
commission under the Ministry of the Interior, a statutory organ of the executive branch. 
However, with the Third Constitutional Amendment, provisions relating to a democratic 
electoral system were introduced into the Constitution as a corrective measure in response to 
the fraudulent election of March 15. The most essential of these was that both the functions of 
election management and the authority to exercise them were assigned to an independent 
constitutional organ that is functionally and organizationally separate from the government. 
Although the Constitution has since undergone several subsequent amendments, the structure 
of placing the election management body as an independent constitutional organ —set out in a 
chapter of the Constitution distinct from that of the government so as to emphasize its 
independence and neutrality —has been consistently maintained through the present 
Constitution.
This reflects a constitutional determination embedded in our constitutional system that, in 
order for the election management body to properly fulfill the constitutional tasks of, inter 
alia, independent and neutral election management required in a representative democracy, it 
must be institutionally shielded from the influence of external power organs such as the 
President, and to that end, the management of elections must be entrusted to an independent 
constitutional organ rather than the executive branch.
3. Whether Respondent has the authority to conduct inspections of official duties with 
respect to Claimant
Article 97 of the Constitution provides that the Board of Audit and Inspection shall be 
established under the President to “audit the settlement of the revenues and expenditures of 
the State and the accounts of the State and the organizations prescribed by statute” and to 
“inspect the official duties of administrative agencies and public officials.” Article 100 of the 
Constitution further provides that, inter alia, the scope of the Board of Audit and Inspection’s 
official duties and the scope of public officials subject to its audit and inspection shall be 
prescribed by statute. Pursuant to these provisions, Article 24 of the Board of Audit and 
Inspection Act sets forth the scope of inspections of official duties, and Article 24, Section (1), 
Item 1 designates as subjects of such inspections “the affairs of administrative agencies 
established under the Government Organization Act or other statutes and the official duties of 
4the public officials belonging thereto.” Ultimately, whether Respondent has the authority to 
conduct inspections of official duties with respect to Claimant depends on whether Claimant 
and its public officials fall within the scope of “administrative agencies and the public 
officials belonging thereto” as prescribed in the constitutional and statutory provisions 
authorizing such inspections —namely, Article 97 of the Constitution and Article 24, Section 
(1), Item 1 of the Board of Audit and Inspection Act.
A. Interpretation of Article 97 of the Constitution 
Under the organizational structure of the Constitution, Respondent is an agency under the 
President and belongs to the executive branch. Article 97 of the Constitution grants 
Respondent the authority to audit the accounts of the “State” and to conduct inspections of 
official duties with respect to “administrative agencies.” While the term “State” as the subject 
of audit can be regarded as encompassing all State organs, the Constitution does not 
specifically define the types of “administrative agencies” subject to inspections of official 
duties.
Respondent contends that, because the election management affairs handled by Claimant 
constitute administrative acts, Claimant falls within the scope of administrative agencies 
subject to inspections of official duties under Article 97 of the Constitution. However, as 
noted above, the constitutional amendment authority, in establishing the election 
commissions as an independent constitutional organ, sought to prevent the government from 
influencing election management and to safeguard its independence and neutrality. Given this, 
Claimant cannot be regarded as automatically falling within the administrative agencies 
subject to inspections of official duties by Respondent, which, under the Constitution, is an 
organ of the executive branch under the President.
Furthermore, Respondent’s authority to conduct inspections of official duties functions as 
an internal control mechanism within the executive branch. Thus, just as other independent 
constitutional organs —such as the National Assembly, the judiciary, and the Constitutional 
Court —are not included among the subjects of Respondent’s inspections of official duties 
under Article 97 of the Constitution, neither are the election commissions, which are likewise 
established as an independent constitutional organ.
5Moreover, Respondent is an administrative agency under the President. In terms of its 
composition, the President appoints both the Chairperson of the Board of Audit and 
Inspection and its commissioners. Article 42, Section (1) of the Board of Audit and 
Inspection Act requires Respondent to report to the President any matters it considers 
significant as a result of its audits and inspections. Under party democracy, the President may 
be a member of a particular political party and have interests closely aligned with that party’s 
policies or advantages. Therefore, if Respondent, which is an agency under the President, 
were permitted to inspect Claimant’s official duties, there would be a risk of undermining 
citizens’ confidence in the fairness and neutrality of election management.
Accordingly, it is reasonable to conclude that Claimant and its public officials are not 
included among the “administrative agencies and public officials” subject to Respondent’s 
inspections of official duties under Article 97 of the Constitution.
B. Interpretation of Article 24, Section (1), Item 1 and Section (3) of the Board of Audit 
and Inspection Act 
As examined above, Claimant and its public officials are not within the scope of 
“administrative agencies and public officials” subject to Respondent’s inspections of official 
duties under Article 97 of the Constitution. Thus, under the principle of interpretation in 
conformity with the Constitution, it is clear that “administrative agencies and the public 
officials belonging thereto” in Article 24, Section (1), Item 1 of the Board of Audit and 
Inspection Act—which concretizes Article 97 of the Constitution —must likewise be 
understood to exclude Claimant and its public officials.
Article 24, Section (3) of the Board of Audit and Inspection Act provides that “The public 
officials referred to in Section (1) shall exclude the public officials belonging to the National 
Assembly, the judiciary, and the Constitutional Court.” This provision does not expressly list 
Claimant’s public officials as being excluded from the target of inspections of official duties. 
However, because, as explained above, the National Assembly, the judiciary, and the 
Constitutional Court —independent constitutional organs separate from the government —are 
not subject to Respondent’s inspections of official duties, Article 24, Section (3)—which 
excludes the public officials of those organs from such inspections —is to be regarded as 
merely illustrative and confirmatory in nature. Thus, the fact that Article 24, Section (3) does 
6not explicitly list Claimant as being excluded from the target of inspections of official duties 
does not alter the conclusion that it is not subject to Respondent’s inspections of official 
duties.
C. Sub-conclusion 
In view of, inter alia, the structure of our Constitution and the interpretation of Article 97 
of the Constitution and Article 24 of the Board of Audit and Inspection Act, Respondent 
cannot be regarded as having been granted the authority to inspect Claimant’s official duties.
4. Whether the Inspections at Issue infringed Claimant’s authority 
Since Respondent has not been vested with the authority to inspect Claimant’s official 
duties, the Inspections at Issue were conducted without constitutional or statutory power, 
thereby infringing Claimant’s authority to independently perform its functions as conferred 
by the Constitution and the Election Commission Act.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
